title: Karenth Dworsky
text:
Dissertation Research



Wildfire fundamentally alters the forest canopy that regulates snowpack energy balance. In the Eastern Cascades, where rain-on-snow events drive some of the most dangerous flood and debris flow hazards, understanding how canopy loss changes snowmelt timing and magnitude is an urgent and open question.
My dissertation uses remote sensing, field instrumentation, and long-term streamflow records to quantify these effects across a gradient of burn severity at two fire sites in Washington State.






Site
Fire
Year
Watershed




Labor Mountain
Labor Mountain Fire
2023
Tillicum Creek


Lower Sugarloaf
Lower Sugarloaf Fire
2024
Peshastin Creek


Squilchuck Creek
—
—
Unburned control

















Topic
Key Data
Status




Paper 1
Does RAVG canopy loss predict spatial snowpack depletion during the December 2025 atmospheric river?
MODIS fSCA · Sentinel-1 SAR · Landsat dNBR · RAVG CC-5
In progress


Paper 2
Regional rain-on-snow detection across the western US
SNOTEL · MODIS · NWM
Planned


Paper 3
Historical burned vs. unburned streamflow response — Eastern Cascades
USGS 15-min streamflow · MTBS 2009–2024
Planned


Paper 4
Field instrumentation and monitoring network design
Timelapse cameras · snow stakes · tipping buckets · pressure transducers
Planned








MODIS fSCA — fractional snow cover area time series
Sentinel-1 SAR — wet snow detection
Landsat dNBR — burn severity mapping
RAVG CC-5 — canopy cover change post-fire
Google Earth Engine — cloud-based processing platform





Field instrumentation deployed at Labor Mountain and Lower Sugarloaf includes timelapse cameras, snow stakes, tipping bucket precipitation gauges, pressure transducers, and temperature/humidity loggers. Reference SNOTEL stations: Pope Ridge, Harts Pass, Rainy Pass, and Blewett Pass.

title: Karenth Dworsky
Karenth Dworsky
text:
Curriculum Vitae



Professional Hydrologist · #24-PH-05033
Steamboat Springs, CO · ORCID
U.S. Citizen · Current Federal Employee: USDA Forest Service, GS-12, Series 1315











PhD, Watershed Science (In Progress)
Colorado State University
Expected 2028 · GPA 3.7



Advisor: Dr. Steven Fassnacht




Post-fire rain-on-snow hydrology, multi-sensor remote sensing (MODIS fSCA, Sentinel-1 SAR)



MS, Geology
University of Utah
2013 · GPA 3.6



Thesis: Subsurface flow modeling and groundwater systems



MS, Environmental Law
University of Auckland, New Zealand
2011 · GPA 3.3



Thesis: Water rights, environmental policy, and transboundary water management



BS, Geological Sciences & Mathematics
University of Alaska Anchorage
2009 · GPA 3.4







Watershed Project Manager, BAER Coordinator & Water Rights Program Manager · 11/2022 – Present
USDA Forest Service, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee, WA · GS-12, Series 1315 · 40 hrs/week

Secured $2–5M in federal restoration funding through GAOA and CFLRP, replacing 8 aquatic organism passages and reconnecting over 50 miles of stream habitat for salmon and native species across a 4-million-acre National Forest
Led watershed restoration planning for Beaver Creek, Teanaway, and Little Naches HUC-8 watersheds, establishing science-based restoration priorities, project sequencing, and long-term monitoring frameworks
Coordinated Burned Area Emergency Response (BAER) following major wildfires (Pioneer, Lower Sugarloaf, Retreat, Labor Mountain), partnering with USGS, WA DNR, and WA Geological Survey to assess debris flow hazards; served as agency public spokesperson on water quality and hazard conditions
Administered federal reserved water rights; represented Forest Service in Yakima Nation and Nooksack water rights adjudications, coordinating with tribal nations, state agencies, and legal counsel
Managed multi-agency restoration programs with 10+ partners including Bureau of Reclamation, Bonneville Power Administration, USFWS, Yakama Nation Fisheries, and Colville Confederated Tribes
Supervised four permanent seasonal staff; developed and delivered technical training programs, weekly internal seminars, and monthly scholarly paper discussions

Hydrologist · 10/2020 – 11/2022
U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Albuquerque District, Albuquerque, NM · GS-11, Series 1315 · 40 hrs/week

Built HEC-HMS and HEC-RAS hydrologic and hydraulic models for water supply planning, flood scenario analysis, and river restoration design for municipal and tribal clients across the Southwest
Developed watershed management plans addressing water quality, flood resilience, and water supply sustainability for tribal nations, El Paso Water, City of Trinidad, and state agencies
Coordinated CWA Section 404 compliance and NEPA documentation; conducted source water protection assessments in watersheds with legacy mine contamination

Hydrologist · 05/2013 – 10/2020
U.S. Geological Survey, Alaska Science Center, Anchorage, AK · GS-09, Series 1315 · 40 hrs/week

Managed statewide water resources monitoring program across Alaska; collected, analyzed, and interpreted streamflow and water quality data from USGS monitoring networks
Secured multi-year cooperative agreement funding from state and federal partners; managed grant budgets and reporting across concurrent awards totaling millions in federal investment
Authored peer-reviewed publications on channel change and bridge scour; developed public data visualization tools to improve Alaskan community access to water resources information
Supervised and trained field technicians across remote Alaska sites; conducted streamflow measurements, water quality sampling, and bridge scour assessments statewide






Dworsky et al. (In Preparation) · Western Snow Conference 2026
Dworsky & Conaway (2019) · USGS Open-File Report 2019-1028
Beebee, Dworsky & Knopp (2019) · USGS Scientific Investigations Report












Category
Tools




Programming
R (advanced) · Google Earth Engine · GitHub


GIS & Remote Sensing
ArcGIS Pro · ArcGIS Online · QGIS · ENVI · Sentinel-1 SAR · MODIS fSCA


Hydrologic Modeling
HEC-RAS · HEC-HMS


Data
SNOTEL · USGS dataRetrieval · ASOS · NHDPlus








Professional Hydrologist (PH) #24-PH-05033 · American Institute of Hydrology
Forest Service Tethered Logging & Soil Impacts Committee
Washington State Post-Fire Hydrology & Atmospheric River Modeling Committee





English (native) · Portuguese (proficient) · Spanish (intermediate)

Full federal resume available upon request.
